text: The following is a case report of a robotic-assisted approach to colocalonic intussusception. Patient is a thirty-six-year-old male with a known cecolipoma currently scheduled for elective resection who presented to the emergency department complaining of worsening lower abdominal pain as well as difficulty passing bowel movements. He denied any blood in his stool, nausea, vomiting, or other obstructive symptoms. Other than his known cecolipoma, he denied any past medical or past surgical history. On exam, he was afebrile with normal vital signs. His abdomen was mildly distended and tender in the right lower quadrant. His labs were within normal limits, including a white count of eight point eight.

He underwent CT scan, which was notable for a segment of ascending colon colonic intussusception. Given these findings, the patient was brought to the operating room for laparoscopic robot assisted right hemicolectomy.

A varus needle is inserted into the left upper quadrant to gain access to the abdomen, followed by the insertion of robotic ports. The surgical robot is docked, and the surgeon uses a small grasping retractor and Cartier instrument with the left hand, while a vessel sealer is operated with the right hand.

The surgeon divides and mobilizes the gastrocolic ligaments at the transverse colon. The hepatic flexure is taken down, and the white line of Toldt is reached and divided, continuing down to the cecum and terminal ileum, fully mobilizing the right colon.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the base of the ileocolic artery, confirming its location with indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence imaging.
The robotic stapler with a white staple cartridge is used to divide the ileocolic at its base.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the mesentery, fully mobilizing ten centimeters of the terminal ileum and separating it from the transverse colon.

Three milliliters of ICG is administered through the patient's peripheral IV, resulting in a vivid green fluorescence of the terminal ileum and transverse colon. Blue staple cartridges on the robotic stapler are used to transect these sections, freeing the entire specimen.

The surgeon performs an intracorporeal isoparastaltic ileocolic anastomosis, aligning the ileum with the transverse colon using two vipral stase deuters. An enterotomy and colotomy are created, and two 45mm blue staple loads are used to form the anastomosis. The common enterocolotomy is closed with a running two-layer suture using a 3-0 V-lock.
The surgical robot is undocked, and the specimen is removed through a small incision at the suprapubic port using a fan and steel instrument.

Final pathology was notable for a lipoma of the ileocecal valve measuring three point eight by three point five by two point two centimeters with acute inflammation and fat necrosis as well as overlying mucosa with focal inflammation, ulceration and ischemic changes. In conclusion, lipomas as the lead point in adult intussusception is rare. Robotic resection and anastomosis is a viable option that can lead to improved outcomes, shorter length of stay, and quicker recovery for the patient.
